_comment: Contribute a help topic by adding a { q, a } entry to "topics". 'a' is markdown: **bold**, *italic*, `code`, [links](https://url) (open in the browser), and 1. numbered lists. Use \n\n between paragraphs.

topics.a:
Each profile holds a Git identity (name + email) and one SSH key. Switching a profile applies it **globally**:

1. Sets `user.name` and `user.email` in your global git config.
2. Points `github.com` at that profile's key in `~/.ssh/config` (inside a managed block, so the rest of your config is untouched).
3. Turns on commit signing with the same key.

So every repo on your machine uses the active profile until you switch again.
When you switch profiles, GitSwitch points git at that profile's SSH key for both **auth** and **commit signing** (`gpg.format=ssh`, `commit.gpgsign=true`). Every commit is then signed automatically.

GitHub stores **Authentication keys** and **Signing keys** separately. Pushing works because your key is registered for auth — but signatures stay “Unverified” until the *same* key is also added as a Signing Key.

1. Open [github.com/settings/ssh/new](https://github.com/settings/ssh/new)
2. Set **Key type → Signing Key** (not Authentication Key).
3. Paste the profile's public key (the same `.pub` you use for auth) and save.

One key covers both roles. Past commits flip to “Verified” retroactively once the Signing Key is added.

topics.q: Why can't it verify my email?
topics.a: The commit's email must be a **verified email** on that GitHub account, or the badge stays “Unverified” even with a valid signature. Add and verify it under [github.com/settings/emails](https://github.com/settings/emails), and make sure the profile's git email matches.

GitSwitch is brand new, and SSH is the simplest, most universal way to manage Git identity — one key handles both auth and commit signing. Support for **GPG keys** and **OAuth/token login** is on the roadmap; SSH was just the right place to start.
